content.problemStatement:
Chef has been observing the stock market closely and wants to maximize his profit by trading a single stock. He is given the prices of the stock over several days.

Chef is allowed to complete **at most two transactions** to earn profit. A transaction consists of **buying** the stock on one day and **selling** it on a later day. Chef must follow a strict rule: **he cannot hold more than one stock at a time**, which means he must sell the current stock before buying again.

Chef wants to determine the **maximum profit** he can achieve under these conditions.

Your task is to help Chef calculate the **highest possible profit** by performing at most two transactions.

Function Declaration

**Function Name**



**calculateMaxProfitcalculateMaxProfitcalculateMaxProfit**



**Parameters**



**stockPricesstockPricesstockPrices** — An array of integers representing the price of the stock on each day.



**Return Value**



Returns an `int` representing the **maximum profit** Chef can achieve.
If no profit is possible, return **000**.


Constraints
1≤T≤1051 \le T \le 10^51≤T≤105
1≤∣stockPrices∣≤1051 \le |stockPrices| \le 10^51≤∣stockPrices∣≤105
0≤stockPrices[i]≤1050 \le stockPrices[i] \le 10^50≤stockPrices[i]≤105
Input Format

The first line contains a single integer **TTT** — the number of test cases.




Each test case consists of:



A line containing an integer **NNN** — the number of days.
A line containing **NNN** space-separated integers representing **stockPricesstockPricesstockPrices**.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single integer — the **maximum profit** Chef can achieve by performing **at most two transactions**.
If no profit can be made, print **000**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the number of days, and the second line contains N space-separated integers representing the stock prices.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single integer representing the maximum profit Chef can achieve by performing at most two transactions.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: best two transactions are buy at 1 sell at 9 (profit 8) and buy at 0 sell at 10 (profit 10) total 18.
Test case 2: prices strictly decrease, no profitable transaction, profit 0.
Test case 3: optimal transactions are buy at 1 sell at 4 (profit 3) and buy at 2 sell at 7 (profit 5) total 8.
All prices are equal, any buy-sell pair yields zero profit, so maximum profit is 0.
Two optimal transactions: buy at 0 sell at 3 (profit 3) and buy at 1 sell at 4 (profit 3) total 6.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using dynamic programming. We maintain four variables: buy1, sell1, buy2, and sell2. buy1 and sell1 represent the maximum profit after the first buy and sell, and buy2 and sell2 represent the maximum profit after the second buy and sell. We update these variables iteratively based on the current stock price.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T * N) total. The precomputation is done once for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the dominant structure is a constant number of variables.

code.Python3:
import sys

def calculate_max_profit(prices):
    buy1 = -10**18
    sell1 = 0
    buy2 = -10**18
    sell2 = 0
    for p in prices:
        buy1 = max(buy1, -p)
        sell1 = max(sell1, buy1 + p)
        buy2 = max(buy2, sell1 - p)
        sell2 = max(sell2, buy2 + p)
    return sell2

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        prices = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        out_lines.append(str(calculate_max_profit(prices)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
